topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard boxes sat stacked in the hallway, a monument to sudden, seismic change. Liam traced a finger over the faded 'SOLD' sticker on a box labeled 'Memories.' Every time he looked at the packed-up life of Maya, a cold, heavy pressure settled in his chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. He watched her packing a ridiculous collection of mismatched mugs, her movements efficient and bright, a stark contrast to the dull ache radiating from his own core. "Across the country," the casual announcement had sounded like a dismissal, a signal that he wasn't important enough to anchor her world. He picked at a loose thread on his worn sweater, the fabric rough against his fingertips. He just stood there, letting the silence of the apartment amplify the hollow echo in his ears, feeling utterly untethered, like a loose piece of driftwood washed out to sea.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually going?" The words felt brittle in Sarah's mouth, thin and sharp. He nodded, a jerky movement that didn't quite reach his eyes. He focused intensely on the pattern of the chipped paint on the windowsill, trying to memorize the imperfections, anything concrete to hold onto. When she finally looked at him, her smile was genuine, brimming with excitement for the new adventure. That light seemed to illuminate everything else in the room—the dusty bookshelf, the unopened mail, the general clutter. He felt a distinct shrinking sensation, as if the space around him was actively closing in. He mumbled something about needing time to process it, the sound barely audible, and then he retreated to the bathroom, leaning against the cool tile, trying to force his heartbeat to slow down, to somehow make himself smaller, less noticeable.

[story 3]

The train station was a cacophony of hurried footsteps and echoing announcements. Alex watched Maya wave frantically from the platform, her silhouette framed against the vast, indifferent sky. He clutched the strap of his backpack so tightly his knuckles turned white. The sheer scale of the departure—the distance, the unknown horizon—made his stomach twist into a tight, nauseous knot. He felt profoundly out of sync with the energy surrounding him; everyone else seemed propelled forward, towards destinations, while he remained rooted, observing the motion. When she finally turned, her eyes searching his face, he managed a weak, practiced grin. "Have the best time," he managed, the words tasting like ash. He didn't feel like a supportive friend; he felt like an observer of someone else’s grand trajectory, a footnote in a story that wasn't his.
